text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic D-III lymph node dissection, carefully preserving the left colic artery and sigmoid artery during the procedure for rectal cancer treatment.
This was a sixty-nine-year-old woman, presented with a two-month history of blood-stained stool.
CT and MRI revealed wall thickening of the rectum, but without mesenteric lymphadenopathy.
Preoperative clinical stage was CT-III-N-O-M-O.
The distance from its distal margin to the anal verge was about five centimeters.
The surgeon performs a careful laparoscopic exploration of the abdominal cavity, inspecting the rectal area and surrounding structures for any abnormalities or signs of cancer progression.
The surgeon dissociates the retroperitoneum from the right side of the mesocolon, carefully separating these anatomical structures during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon enters the Toltz gap, a space between the mesocolon and retroperitoneum, using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon enters the Toltz gap along the right side of the inferior mesenteric artery, navigating the anatomical space during the procedure.
The surgeon performs an apical lymph node dissection, targeting lymph node number 253, using precise surgical techniques.
The surgeon performs a dissection of the apical lymph node number 253, focusing on precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissociates and exposes the inferior mesenteric artery, inferior mesenteric vein, and left colic artery, carefully separating these vascular structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs dissection of apical lymph node number 253, focusing on precise separation of the lymph node from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs dissection of apical lymph node number 253, focusing on precise separation and exposure of the node within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissociates and exposes the inferior mesenteric vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon ligates and incises the branches of the inferior mesenteric vein, taking care to protect the adjacent left colic artery.
The surgeon ligates and incises the inferior mesenteric vein, ensuring the vessel is securely closed and separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissociates and exposes the first sigmoid artery, then ligates and incises the inferior mesenteric artery below the left colic artery.
The surgeon carefully protects the left ureter and gonadal vessels, ensuring these structures are not damaged during the procedure.
The surgeon places a gauze pad to protect the left ureter and gonadal vessels, ensuring these structures are shielded during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully separates adhesions around the sigmoid colon, revealing the underlying tissue and anatomical structures.
The surgeon dissociates the sigmoid mesocolon, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to facilitate further surgical access.
The surgeon dissociates the retro-rectal space, carefully separating the tissue layers in the posterior region of the rectum.
The surgeon dissociates the right pararectal space, carefully separating the tissue in this area.
The surgeon dissociates the left pararectal space, carefully separating the tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon dissociates and exposes the right intestinal wall, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissociates and exposes the anterior rectal wall, revealing the underlying tissue layers and anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissociates and exposes the left intestinal wall, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue planes in the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the rectum at the pre-resect position, preparing for resection by cutting through the rectal tissue.
The surgeon incises the sigmoid mesocolon, revealing underlying structures and facilitating further dissection in the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through the incision made for the protective ileostomy, carefully removing the tissue from the surgical site.
The surgeon performs an enteric and intestinal anastomosis, joining the intestinal segments with sutures or staples to restore continuity after resection.
The surgeon sutures and strengthens the anastomosis site, ensuring secure connection between the intestinal segments.
The surgeon places a drainage tube in the surgical site, ensuring proper positioning for fluid drainage post-procedure.
The surgeon creates a stoma at the site of the incision, manipulating the surrounding tissue to establish an opening for the stoma.
The surgeon displays the excised specimen, which is a total mesorectal excision, following the extraction from the protective ileostomy incision.
The pathologic staging was PT-III-N-I-M-Zero, with one apical lymph node metastasis.
